{{ill|Nose Highway|ja|能勢街道|en}} [[Image: Nose kaido&Harada shrine.jpg|thumbnail|240px|Nose Kaidō near Okamachi, which has become a shopping street. To the left is Harada Shrine.]] The '''Nose Kaidō''' (能勢街道) is an old highway stretching from {{ill|Nakatsu, Kita, Osaka|ja|中津 (大阪市)}} in [[Osaka]]'s Kita Ward, passing through {{ill|Ikeda, Osaka|ja|池田市}}, and ending at {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, where the {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} is located. From Chōkōji-minami, the highway splits, with the {{ill|Minoh Kaidō|ja|箕面街道}} providing a pilgrimage route to sites such as {{ill|Minoh Waterfall|ja|箕面の滝}}, {{ill|Ryuan-ji (Minoh)|ja|瀧安寺}}, and {{ill|Katsuo-ji|ja|勝尾寺}}. Today, the old road name is also used colloquially for the routes of {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} (Osaka–Toyonaka–Ikeda) and {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} (Ikeda–Tada–Nose–Sasayama), excluding bypasses. == History and Geography == The Nose Kaidō diverged from the {{ill|Chūgoku Kaidō|ja|中国街道}} in Nakatsu, then passed through {{ill|Jūso|ja|十三 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Mikuni, Osaka|ja|三国 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Shōnai, Toyonaka|ja|庄内 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Okamachi, Toyonaka|ja|岡町 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}}, Ikeda, Kibe, Furue, {{ill|Tada Station (Hyogo)|ja|多田駅 (兵庫県)}}, and {{ill|Ichinotorii Station|ja|一の鳥居駅}}, before following the ridgeline to Myōken Mountain. This highway facilitated the transportation of goods such as sake, textiles, and timber from Ikeda and Nose to Osaka. Additionally, rice, chestnuts, charcoal, silver, and copper from {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}} were carried along this route. Many significant religious sites lined the highway, including {{ill|Hattori Tenjingu Shrine|ja|服部天神宮}}, {{ill|Tōkō-in (Toyonaka)|ja|東光院 (豊中市)}} (Hagi Temple), {{ill|Harada Shrine (Toyonaka)|ja|原田神社}}, and {{ill|Tada Shrine|ja|多田神社}}. Other sites, such as {{ill|Nakayama-dera|ja|中山寺}} and Katsuo-ji, located off the main route, also attracted pilgrims. In 1876, the Nose Kaidō was designated as one of three first-class main roads in Osaka Prefecture under Daijōkan Order No. 60, which established road classifications. Historically, the route between Okamachi and Ikeda passed through Mount Tonoyama (south of Osaka University's Toyonaka Campus). Due to the steep incline, which hampered the transport of goods, a flatter "New Nose Kaidō" was built in 1894, running from the vicinity of present-day {{ill|Hankyu Toyonaka Station|ja|阪急豊中駅}} to Ishibashi. This new route closely aligns with today’s National Route 176. Modern infrastructure, such as {{ill|Hankyu Electric Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}'s {{ill|Hankyu Takarazuka Line|ja|阪急宝塚本線}} and National Routes 176 and 173, run parallel to the old road. The {{ill|Nose Electric Railway|ja|能勢電鉄}}'s {{ill|Nose Electric Railway Myōken Line|ja|能勢電鉄妙見線}} also parallels the highway north of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyogo|ja|川西市}}. These developments led to the decline of the old highway, though much of its path remains as residential roads. Commercial hubs such as Okamachi and the area around {{ill|Ishibashi Handai-mae Station|ja|石橋阪大前駅}} have preserved the historical route as shopping streets. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')
({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == Related Topics == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Historic Roads]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Hyogo]] [[Category:Roads in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Roads in Hyogo Prefecture]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Nose Highway]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[ja:能勢街道]]

Today, the old road name is also used colloquially for the routes of {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} (Osaka–Toyonaka–Ikeda) and {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} (Ikeda–Tada–Nose–Sasayama), excluding bypasses. == History and Geography == The Nose Kaidō diverged from the {{ill|Chūgoku Kaidō|ja|中国街道}} in Nakatsu, then passed through {{ill|Jūso|ja|十三 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Mikuni, Osaka|ja|三国 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Shōnai, Toyonaka|ja|庄内 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Okamachi, Toyonaka|ja|岡町 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}}, Ikeda, Kibe, Furue, {{ill|Tada Station (Hyogo)|ja|多田駅 (兵庫県)}}, and {{ill|Ichinotorii Station|ja|一の鳥居駅}}, before following the ridgeline to Myōken Mountain. This highway facilitated the transportation of goods such as sake, textiles, and timber from Ikeda and Nose to Osaka. Additionally, rice, chestnuts, charcoal, silver, and copper from {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}} were carried along this route. Many significant religious sites lined the highway, including {{ill|Hattori Tenjingu Shrine|ja|服部天神宮}}, {{ill|Tōkō-in (Toyonaka)|ja|東光院 (豊中市)}} (Hagi Temple), {{ill|Harada Shrine (Toyonaka)|ja|原田神社}}, and {{ill|Tada Shrine|ja|多田神社}}. Other sites, such as {{ill|Nakayama-dera|ja|中山寺}} and Katsuo-ji, located off the main route, also attracted pilgrims. In 1876, the Nose Kaidō was designated as one of three first-class main roads in Osaka Prefecture under Daijōkan Order No. 60, which established road classifications. Historically, the route between Okamachi and Ikeda passed through Mount Tonoyama (south of Osaka University's Toyonaka Campus). Due to the steep incline, which hampered the transport of goods, a flatter "New Nose Kaidō" was built in 1894, running from the vicinity of present-day {{ill|Hankyu Toyonaka Station|ja|阪急豊中駅}} to Ishibashi. This new route closely aligns with today’s National Route 176. Modern infrastructure, such as {{ill|Hankyu Electric Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}'s {{ill|Hankyu Takarazuka Line|ja|阪急宝塚本線}} and National Routes 176 and 173, run parallel to the old road. The {{ill|Nose Electric Railway|ja|能勢電鉄}}'s {{ill|Nose Electric Railway Myōken Line|ja|能勢電鉄妙見線}} also parallels the highway north of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyogo|ja|川西市}}. These developments led to the decline of the old highway, though much of its path remains as residential roads. Commercial hubs such as Okamachi and the area around {{ill|Ishibashi Handai-mae Station|ja|石橋阪大前駅}} have preserved the historical route as shopping streets. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')
({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == Related Topics == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Historic Roads]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Hyogo]] [[Category:Roads in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Roads in Hyogo Prefecture]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Nose Highway-qq]] [[Category:Nose Highway]][[ja:能勢街道]]

{{ill|Nose Highway|ja|能勢街道|en}} [[Image: Nose kaido&Harada shrine.jpg|thumbnail|240px|Nose Kaidō near Okamachi, which has become a shopping street. To the left is Harada Shrine.]] The '''Nose Kaidō''' (能勢街道) is an old highway stretching from {{ill|Nakatsu, Kita, Osaka|ja|中津 (大阪市)}} in [[Osaka]]'s Kita Ward, passing through {{ill|Ikeda, Osaka|ja|池田市}}, and ending at {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, where the {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} is located. From Chōkōji-minami, the highway splits, with the {{ill|Minoh Kaidō|ja|箕面街道}} providing a pilgrimage route to sites such as {{ill|Minoh Waterfall|ja|箕面の滝}}, {{ill|Ryuan-ji (Minoh)|ja|瀧安寺}}, and {{ill|Katsuo-ji|ja|勝尾寺}}. Today, the old road name is also used colloquially for the routes of {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} (Osaka–Toyonaka–Ikeda) and {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} (Ikeda–Tada–Nose–Sasayama), excluding bypasses. == History and Geography == The Nose Kaidō diverged from the {{ill|Chūgoku Kaidō|ja|中国街道}} in Nakatsu, then passed through {{ill|Jūso|ja|十三 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Mikuni, Osaka|ja|三国 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Shōnai, Toyonaka|ja|庄内 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Okamachi, Toyonaka|ja|岡町 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}}, Ikeda, Kibe, Furue, {{ill|Tada Station (Hyogo)|ja|多田駅 (兵庫県)}}, and {{ill|Ichinotorii Station|ja|一の鳥居駅}}, before following the ridgeline to Myōken Mountain. This highway facilitated the transportation of goods such as sake, textiles, and timber from Ikeda and Nose to Osaka. Additionally, rice, chestnuts, charcoal, silver, and copper from {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}} were carried along this route. Many significant religious sites lined the highway, including {{ill|Hattori Tenjingu Shrine|ja|服部天神宮}}, {{ill|Tōkō-in (Toyonaka)|ja|東光院 (豊中市)}} (Hagi Temple), {{ill|Harada Shrine (Toyonaka)|ja|原田神社}}, and {{ill|Tada Shrine|ja|多田神社}}. Other sites, such as {{ill|Nakayama-dera|ja|中山寺}} and Katsuo-ji, located off the main route, also attracted pilgrims. In 1876, the Nose Kaidō was designated as one of three first-class main roads in Osaka Prefecture under Daijōkan Order No. 60, which established road classifications. Historically, the route between Okamachi and Ikeda passed through Mount Tonoyama (south of Osaka University's Toyonaka Campus). Due to the steep incline, which hampered the transport of goods, a flatter "New Nose Kaidō" was built in 1894, running from the vicinity of present-day {{ill|Hankyu Toyonaka Station|ja|阪急豊中駅}} to Ishibashi. This new route closely aligns with today’s National Route 176. Modern infrastructure, such as {{ill|Hankyu Electric Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}'s {{ill|Hankyu Takarazuka Line|ja|阪急宝塚本線}} and National Routes 176 and 173, run parallel to the old road. The {{ill|Nose Electric Railway|ja|能勢電鉄}}'s {{ill|Nose Electric Railway Myōken Line|ja|能勢電鉄妙見線}} also parallels the highway north of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyogo|ja|川西市}}. These developments led to the decline of the old highway, though much of its path remains as residential roads. Commercial hubs such as Okamachi and the area around {{ill|Ishibashi Handai-mae Station|ja|石橋阪大前駅}} have preserved the historical route as shopping streets. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')
({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == Related Topics == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Historic Roads]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Hyogo]] [[Category:Roads in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Roads in Hyogo Prefecture]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Nose Highway-qq]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:能勢街道]]

These developments led to the decline of the old highway, though much of its path remains as residential roads. Commercial hubs such as Okamachi and the area around {{ill|Ishibashi Handai-mae Station|ja|石橋阪大前駅}} have preserved the historical route as shopping streets. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')

It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == Related Topics == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Historic Roads]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Hyogo]] [[Category:Roads in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Roads in Hyogo Prefecture]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Nose Highway-qq]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:能勢街道]]

{{ill|Nose Highway|ja|能勢街道|en}} [[Image: Nose kaido&Harada shrine.jpg|thumbnail|240px|Nose Kaidō near Okamachi, which has become a shopping street. To the left is Harada Shrine.]] The '''Nose Kaidō''' (能勢街道) is an old highway stretching from {{ill|Nakatsu, Kita, Osaka|ja|中津 (大阪市)}} in [[Osaka]]'s Kita Ward, passing through {{ill|Ikeda, Osaka|ja|池田市}}, and ending at {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, where the {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} is located. From Chōkōji-minami, the highway splits, with the {{ill|Minoh Kaidō|ja|箕面街道}} providing a pilgrimage route to sites such as {{ill|Minoh Waterfall|ja|箕面の滝}}, {{ill|Ryuan-ji (Minoh)|ja|瀧安寺}}, and {{ill|Katsuo-ji|ja|勝尾寺}}. Today, the old road name is also used colloquially for the routes of {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} (Osaka–Toyonaka–Ikeda) and {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} (Ikeda–Tada–Nose–Sasayama), excluding bypasses. == History and Geography == The Nose Kaidō diverged from the {{ill|Chūgoku Kaidō|ja|中国街道}} in Nakatsu, then passed through {{ill|Jūso|ja|十三 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Mikuni, Osaka|ja|三国 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Shōnai, Toyonaka|ja|庄内 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Okamachi, Toyonaka|ja|岡町 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}}, Ikeda, Kibe, Furue, {{ill|Tada Station (Hyogo)|ja|多田駅 (兵庫県)}}, and {{ill|Ichinotorii Station|ja|一の鳥居駅}}, before following the ridgeline to Myōken Mountain. This highway facilitated the transportation of goods such as sake, textiles, and timber from Ikeda and Nose to Osaka. Additionally, rice, chestnuts, charcoal, silver, and copper from {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}} were carried along this route. Many significant religious sites lined the highway, including {{ill|Hattori Tenjingu Shrine|ja|服部天神宮}}, {{ill|Tōkō-in (Toyonaka)|ja|東光院 (豊中市)}} (Hagi Temple), {{ill|Harada Shrine (Toyonaka)|ja|原田神社}}, and {{ill|Tada Shrine|ja|多田神社}}. Other sites, such as {{ill|Nakayama-dera|ja|中山寺}} and Katsuo-ji, located off the main route, also attracted pilgrims. In 1876, the Nose Kaidō was designated as one of three first-class main roads in Osaka Prefecture under Daijōkan Order No. 60, which established road classifications. Historically, the route between Okamachi and Ikeda passed through Mount Tonoyama (south of Osaka University's Toyonaka Campus). Due to the steep incline, which hampered the transport of goods, a flatter "New Nose Kaidō" was built in 1894, running from the vicinity of present-day {{ill|Hankyu Toyonaka Station|ja|阪急豊中駅}} to Ishibashi. This new route closely aligns with today’s National Route 176. Modern infrastructure, such as {{ill|Hankyu Electric Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}'s {{ill|Hankyu Takarazuka Line|ja|阪急宝塚本線}} and National Routes 176 and 173, run parallel to the old road. The {{ill|Nose Electric Railway|ja|能勢電鉄}}'s {{ill|Nose Electric Railway Myōken Line|ja|能勢電鉄妙見線}} also parallels the highway north of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyogo|ja|川西市}}. These developments led to the decline of the old highway, though much of its path remains as residential roads. Commercial hubs such as Okamachi and the area around {{ill|Ishibashi Handai-mae Station|ja|石橋阪大前駅}} have preserved the historical route as shopping streets. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')
({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == Related Topics == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Historic Roads]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Hyogo]] [[Category:Roads in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Roads in Hyogo Prefecture]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Nose Highway-qq]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:能勢街道]]

Today, the old road name is also used colloquially for the routes of {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} (Osaka–Toyonaka–Ikeda) and {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} (Ikeda–Tada–Nose–Sasayama), excluding bypasses. == History and Geography == The Nose Kaidō diverged from the {{ill|Chūgoku Kaidō|ja|中国街道}} in Nakatsu, then passed through {{ill|Jūso|ja|十三 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Mikuni, Osaka|ja|三国 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Shōnai, Toyonaka|ja|庄内 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Okamachi, Toyonaka|ja|岡町 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}}, Ikeda, Kibe, Furue, {{ill|Tada Station (Hyogo)|ja|多田駅 (兵庫県)}}, and {{ill|Ichinotorii Station|ja|一の鳥居駅}}, before following the ridgeline to Myōken Mountain. This highway facilitated the transportation of goods such as sake, textiles, and timber from Ikeda and Nose to Osaka. Additionally, rice, chestnuts, charcoal, silver, and copper from {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}} were carried along this route. Many significant religious sites lined the highway, including {{ill|Hattori Tenjingu Shrine|ja|服部天神宮}}, {{ill|Tōkō-in (Toyonaka)|ja|東光院 (豊中市)}} (Hagi Temple), {{ill|Harada Shrine (Toyonaka)|ja|原田神社}}, and {{ill|Tada Shrine|ja|多田神社}}. Other sites, such as {{ill|Nakayama-dera|ja|中山寺}} and Katsuo-ji, located off the main route, also attracted pilgrims. In 1876, the Nose Kaidō was designated as one of three first-class main roads in Osaka Prefecture under Daijōkan Order No. 60, which established road classifications. Historically, the route between Okamachi and Ikeda passed through Mount Tonoyama (south of Osaka University's Toyonaka Campus). Due to the steep incline, which hampered the transport of goods, a flatter "New Nose Kaidō" was built in 1894, running from the vicinity of present-day {{ill|Hankyu Toyonaka Station|ja|阪急豊中駅}} to Ishibashi. This new route closely aligns with today’s National Route 176. Modern infrastructure, such as {{ill|Hankyu Electric Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}'s {{ill|Hankyu Takarazuka Line|ja|阪急宝塚本線}} and National Routes 176 and 173, run parallel to the old road. The {{ill|Nose Electric Railway|ja|能勢電鉄}}'s {{ill|Nose Electric Railway Myōken Line|ja|能勢電鉄妙見線}} also parallels the highway north of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyogo|ja|川西市}}. These developments led to the decline of the old highway, though much of its path remains as residential roads. Commercial hubs such as Okamachi and the area around {{ill|Ishibashi Handai-mae Station|ja|石橋阪大前駅}} have preserved the historical route as shopping streets. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')
({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == Related Topics == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Historic Roads]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Hyogo]] [[Category:Roads in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Roads in Hyogo Prefecture]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Nose Highway-qq]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:能勢街道]]

Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')
({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == Related Topics == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Historic Roads]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Hyogo]] [[Category:Roads in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Roads in Hyogo Prefecture]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Nose Highway-qq]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[ja:能勢街道]]

{{ill|Nose Highway|ja|能勢街道|en}} [[Image: Nose kaido&Harada shrine.jpg|thumbnail|240px|Nose Kaidō near Okamachi, which has become a shopping street. To the left is Harada Shrine.]] The '''Nose Kaidō''' (能勢街道) is an old highway stretching from {{ill|Nakatsu, Kita, Osaka|ja|中津 (大阪市)}} in [[Osaka]]'s Kita Ward, passing through {{ill|Ikeda, Osaka|ja|池田市}}, and ending at {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, where the {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} is located. From Chōkōji-minami, the highway splits, with the {{ill|Minoh Kaidō|ja|箕面街道}} providing a pilgrimage route to sites such as {{ill|Minoh Waterfall|ja|箕面の滝}}, {{ill|Ryuan-ji (Minoh)|ja|瀧安寺}}, and {{ill|Katsuo-ji|ja|勝尾寺}}. Today, the old road name is also used colloquially for the routes of {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} (Osaka–Toyonaka–Ikeda) and {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} (Ikeda–Tada–Nose–Sasayama), excluding bypasses. == History and Geography == The Nose Kaidō diverged from the {{ill|Chūgoku Kaidō|ja|中国街道}} in Nakatsu, then passed through {{ill|Jūso|ja|十三 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Mikuni, Osaka|ja|三国 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Shōnai, Toyonaka|ja|庄内 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Okamachi, Toyonaka|ja|岡町 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}}, Ikeda, Kibe, Furue, {{ill|Tada Station (Hyogo)|ja|多田駅 (兵庫県)}}, and {{ill|Ichinotorii Station|ja|一の鳥居駅}}, before following the ridgeline to Myōken Mountain. This highway facilitated the transportation of goods such as sake, textiles, and timber from Ikeda and Nose to Osaka. Additionally, rice, chestnuts, charcoal, silver, and copper from {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}} were carried along this route. Many significant religious sites lined the highway, including {{ill|Hattori Tenjingu Shrine|ja|服部天神宮}}, {{ill|Tōkō-in (Toyonaka)|ja|東光院 (豊中市)}} (Hagi Temple), {{ill|Harada Shrine (Toyonaka)|ja|原田神社}}, and {{ill|Tada Shrine|ja|多田神社}}. Other sites, such as {{ill|Nakayama-dera|ja|中山寺}} and Katsuo-ji, located off the main route, also attracted pilgrims. In 1876, the Nose Kaidō was designated as one of three first-class main roads in Osaka Prefecture under Daijōkan Order No. 60, which established road classifications. Historically, the route between Okamachi and Ikeda passed through Mount Tonoyama (south of Osaka University's Toyonaka Campus). Due to the steep incline, which hampered the transport of goods, a flatter "New Nose Kaidō" was built in 1894, running from the vicinity of present-day {{ill|Hankyu Toyonaka Station|ja|阪急豊中駅}} to Ishibashi. This new route closely aligns with today’s National Route 176. Modern infrastructure, such as {{ill|Hankyu Electric Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}'s {{ill|Hankyu Takarazuka Line|ja|阪急宝塚本線}} and National Routes 176 and 173, run parallel to the old road. The {{ill|Nose Electric Railway|ja|能勢電鉄}}'s {{ill|Nose Electric Railway Myōken Line|ja|能勢電鉄妙見線}} also parallels the highway north of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyogo|ja|川西市}}. These developments led to the decline of the old highway, though much of its path remains as residential roads. Commercial hubs such as Okamachi and the area around {{ill|Ishibashi Handai-mae Station|ja|石橋阪大前駅}} have preserved the historical route as shopping streets. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')
({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == Related Topics == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Historic Roads]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Hyogo]] [[Category:Roads in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Roads in Hyogo Prefecture]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Nose Highway-qq]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[ja:能勢街道]]

{{ill|Nose Highway|ja|能勢街道|en}} [[Image: Nose kaido&Harada shrine.jpg|thumbnail|240px|Nose Kaidō near Okamachi, which has become a shopping street. To the left is Harada Shrine.]] The '''Nose Kaidō''' (能勢街道) is an old highway stretching from {{ill|Nakatsu, Kita, Osaka|ja|中津 (大阪市)}} in [[Osaka]]'s Kita Ward, passing through {{ill|Ikeda, Osaka|ja|池田市}}, and ending at {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, where the {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} is located. From Chōkōji-minami, the highway splits, with the {{ill|Minoh Kaidō|ja|箕面街道}} providing a pilgrimage route to sites such as {{ill|Minoh Waterfall|ja|箕面の滝}}, {{ill|Ryuan-ji (Minoh)|ja|瀧安寺}}, and {{ill|Katsuo-ji|ja|勝尾寺}}. Today, the old road name is also used colloquially for the routes of {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} (Osaka–Toyonaka–Ikeda) and {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} (Ikeda–Tada–Nose–Sasayama), excluding bypasses. == History and Geography == The Nose Kaidō diverged from the {{ill|Chūgoku Kaidō|ja|中国街道}} in Nakatsu, then passed through {{ill|Jūso|ja|十三 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Mikuni, Osaka|ja|三国 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Shōnai, Toyonaka|ja|庄内 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Okamachi, Toyonaka|ja|岡町 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}}, Ikeda, Kibe, Furue, {{ill|Tada Station (Hyogo)|ja|多田駅 (兵庫県)}}, and {{ill|Ichinotorii Station|ja|一の鳥居駅}}, before following the ridgeline to Myōken Mountain. This highway facilitated the transportation of goods such as sake, textiles, and timber from Ikeda and Nose to Osaka. Additionally, rice, chestnuts, charcoal, silver, and copper from {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}} were carried along this route. Many significant religious sites lined the highway, including {{ill|Hattori Tenjingu Shrine|ja|服部天神宮}}, {{ill|Tōkō-in (Toyonaka)|ja|東光院 (豊中市)}} (Hagi Temple), {{ill|Harada Shrine (Toyonaka)|ja|原田神社}}, and {{ill|Tada Shrine|ja|多田神社}}. Other sites, such as {{ill|Nakayama-dera|ja|中山寺}} and Katsuo-ji, located off the main route, also attracted pilgrims. In 1876, the Nose Kaidō was designated as one of three first-class main roads in Osaka Prefecture under Daijōkan Order No. 60, which established road classifications. Historically, the route between Okamachi and Ikeda passed through Mount Tonoyama (south of Osaka University's Toyonaka Campus). Due to the steep incline, which hampered the transport of goods, a flatter "New Nose Kaidō" was built in 1894, running from the vicinity of present-day {{ill|Hankyu Toyonaka Station|ja|阪急豊中駅}} to Ishibashi. This new route closely aligns with today’s National Route 176. Modern infrastructure, such as {{ill|Hankyu Electric Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}'s {{ill|Hankyu Takarazuka Line|ja|阪急宝塚本線}} and National Routes 176 and 173, run parallel to the old road. The {{ill|Nose Electric Railway|ja|能勢電鉄}}'s {{ill|Nose Electric Railway Myōken Line|ja|能勢電鉄妙見線}} also parallels the highway north of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyogo|ja|川西市}}. These developments led to the decline of the old highway, though much of its path remains as residential roads. Commercial hubs such as Okamachi and the area around {{ill|Ishibashi Handai-mae Station|ja|石橋阪大前駅}} have preserved the historical route as shopping streets. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')
({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == See Also == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Historic Roads]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Hyogo]] [[Category:Roads in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Roads in Hyogo Prefecture]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Nose Highway-->[[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[ja:能勢街道]]

This highway facilitated the transportation of goods such as sake, textiles, and timber from Ikeda and Nose to Osaka. Additionally, rice, chestnuts, charcoal, silver, and copper from {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}} were carried along this route. Many significant religious sites lined the highway, including {{ill|Hattori Tenjingu Shrine|ja|服部天神宮}}, {{ill|Tōkō-in (Toyonaka)|ja|東光院 (豊中市)}} (Hagi Temple), {{ill|Harada Shrine (Toyonaka)|ja|原田神社}}, and {{ill|Tada Shrine|ja|多田神社}}. Other sites, such as {{ill|Nakayama-dera|ja|中山寺}} and Katsuo-ji, located off the main route, also attracted pilgrims. In 1876, the Nose Kaidō was designated as one of three first-class main roads in Osaka Prefecture under Daijōkan Order No. 60, which established road classifications. Historically, the route between Okamachi and Ikeda passed through Mount Tonoyama (south of Osaka University's Toyonaka Campus). Due to the steep incline, which hampered the transport of goods, a flatter "New Nose Kaidō" was built in 1894, running from the vicinity of present-day {{ill|Hankyu Toyonaka Station|ja|阪急豊中駅}} to Ishibashi. This new route closely aligns with today’s National Route 176. Modern infrastructure, such as {{ill|Hankyu Electric Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}'s {{ill|Hankyu Takarazuka Line|ja|阪急宝塚本線}} and National Routes 176 and 173, run parallel to the old road. The {{ill|Nose Electric Railway|ja|能勢電鉄}}'s {{ill|Nose Electric Railway Myōken Line|ja|能勢電鉄妙見線}} also parallels the highway north of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyogo|ja|川西市}}. These developments led to the decline of the old highway, though much of its path remains as residential roads. Commercial hubs such as Okamachi and the area around {{ill|Ishibashi Handai-mae Station|ja|石橋阪大前駅}} have preserved the historical route as shopping streets. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')
({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == See Also == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Historic Roads]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Hyogo]] [[Category:Roads in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Roads in Hyogo Prefecture]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[ja:能勢街道]]

({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == See Also == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Historic Roads]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Hyogo]] [[Category:Roads in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Roads in Hyogo Prefecture]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:suQqqqqq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[ja:能勢街道]]

{{ill|Nose Highway|ja|能勢街道|en}} [[Image: Nose kaido&Harada shrine.jpg|thumbnail|240px|Nose Kaidō near Okamachi, which has become a shopping street. To the left is Harada Shrine.]] The '''Nose Kaidō''' (能勢街道) is an old highway stretching from {{ill|Nakatsu, Kita, Osaka|ja|中津 (大阪市)}} in [[Osaka]]'s Kita Ward, passing through {{ill|Ikeda, Osaka|ja|池田市}}, and ending at {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, where the {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} is located. From Chōkōji-minami, the highway splits, with the {{ill|Minoh Kaidō|ja|箕面街道}} providing a pilgrimage route to sites such as {{ill|Minoh Waterfall|ja|箕面の滝}}, {{ill|Ryuan-ji (Minoh)|ja|瀧安寺}}, and {{ill|Katsuo-ji|ja|勝尾寺}}. Today, the old road name is also used colloquially for the routes of {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} (Osaka–Toyonaka–Ikeda) and {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} (Ikeda–Tada–Nose–Sasayama), excluding bypasses. == History and Geography == The Nose Kaidō diverged from the {{ill|Chūgoku Kaidō|ja|中国街道}} in Nakatsu, then passed through {{ill|Jūso|ja|十三 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Mikuni, Osaka|ja|三国 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Shōnai, Toyonaka|ja|庄内 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Okamachi, Toyonaka|ja|岡町 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}}, Ikeda, Kibe, Furue, {{ill|Tada Station (Hyogo)|ja|多田駅 (兵庫県)}}, and {{ill|Ichinotorii Station|ja|一の鳥居駅}}, before following the ridgeline to Myōken Mountain. This highway facilitated the transportation of goods such as sake, textiles, and timber from Ikeda and Nose to Osaka. Additionally, rice, chestnuts, charcoal, silver, and copper from {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}} were carried along this route. Many significant religious sites lined the highway, including {{ill|Hattori Tenjingu Shrine|ja|服部天神宮}}, {{ill|Tōkō-in (Toyonaka)|ja|東光院 (豊中市)}} (Hagi Temple), {{ill|Harada Shrine (Toyonaka)|ja|原田神社}}, and {{ill|Tada Shrine|ja|多田神社}}. Other sites, such as {{ill|Nakayama-dera|ja|中山寺}} and Katsuo-ji, located off the main route, also attracted pilgrims. In 1876, the Nose Kaidō was designated as one of three first-class main roads in Osaka Prefecture under Daijōkan Order No. 60, which established road classifications. Historically, the route between Okamachi and Ikeda passed through Mount Tonoyama (south of Osaka University's Toyonaka Campus). Due to the steep incline, which hampered the transport of goods, a flatter "New Nose Kaidō" was built in 1894, running from the vicinity of present-day {{ill|Hankyu Toyonaka Station|ja|阪急豊中駅}} to Ishibashi. This new route closely aligns with today’s National Route 176. Modern infrastructure, such as {{ill|Hankyu Electric Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}'s {{ill|Hankyu Takarazuka Line|ja|阪急宝塚本線}} and National Routes 176 and 173, run parallel to the old road. The {{ill|Nose Electric Railway|ja|能勢電鉄}}'s {{ill|Nose Electric Railway Myōken Line|ja|能勢電鉄妙見線}} also parallels the highway north of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyogo|ja|川西市}}. These developments led to the decline of the old highway, though much of its path remains as residential roads. Commercial hubs such as Okamachi and the area around {{ill|Ishibashi Handai-mae Station|ja|石橋阪大前駅}} have preserved the historical route as shopping streets. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')
({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == See Also == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Historic Roads]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Hyogo]] [[Category:Roads in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Roads in Hyogo Prefecture]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:suQqqq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[ja:能勢街道]]

Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == See Also == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Historic Roads]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Hyogo]] [[Category:Roads in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Roads in Hyogo Prefecture]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:suQqqq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:能勢街道]]

{{ill|Nose Highway|ja|能勢街道|en}} [[Image: Nose kaido&Harada shrine.jpg|thumbnail|240px|Nose Kaidō near Okamachi, which has become a shopping street. To the left is Harada Shrine.]] The '''Nose Kaidō''' (能勢街道) is an old highway stretching from {{ill|Nakatsu, Kita, Osaka|ja|中津 (大阪市)}} in [[Osaka]]'s Kita Ward, passing through {{ill|Ikeda, Osaka|ja|池田市}}, and ending at {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, where the {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} is located. From Chōkōji-minami, the highway splits, with the {{ill|Minoh Kaidō|ja|箕面街道}} providing a pilgrimage route to sites such as {{ill|Minoh Waterfall|ja|箕面の滝}}, {{ill|Ryuan-ji (Minoh)|ja|瀧安寺}}, and {{ill|Katsuo-ji|ja|勝尾寺}}. Today, the old road name is also used colloquially for the routes of {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} (Osaka–Toyonaka–Ikeda) and {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} (Ikeda–Tada–Nose–Sasayama), excluding bypasses. == History and Geography == The Nose Kaidō diverged from the {{ill|Chūgoku Kaidō|ja|中国街道}} in Nakatsu, then passed through {{ill|Jūso|ja|十三 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Mikuni, Osaka|ja|三国 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Shōnai, Toyonaka|ja|庄内 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Okamachi, Toyonaka|ja|岡町 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}}, Ikeda, Kibe, Furue, {{ill|Tada Station (Hyogo)|ja|多田駅 (兵庫県)}}, and {{ill|Ichinotorii Station|ja|一の鳥居駅}}, before following the ridgeline to Myōken Mountain. This highway facilitated the transportation of goods such as sake, textiles, and timber from Ikeda and Nose to Osaka. Additionally, rice, chestnuts, charcoal, silver, and copper from {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}} were carried along this route. Many significant religious sites lined the highway, including {{ill|Hattori Tenjingu Shrine|ja|服部天神宮}}, {{ill|Tōkō-in (Toyonaka)|ja|東光院 (豊中市)}} (Hagi Temple), {{ill|Harada Shrine (Toyonaka)|ja|原田神社}}, and {{ill|Tada Shrine|ja|多田神社}}. Other sites, such as {{ill|Nakayama-dera|ja|中山寺}} and Katsuo-ji, located off the main route, also attracted pilgrims. In 1876, the Nose Kaidō was designated as one of three first-class main roads in Osaka Prefecture under Daijōkan Order No. 60, which established road classifications. Historically, the route between Okamachi and Ikeda passed through Mount Tonoyama (south of Osaka University's Toyonaka Campus). Due to the steep incline, which hampered the transport of goods, a flatter "New Nose Kaidō" was built in 1894, running from the vicinity of present-day {{ill|Hankyu Toyonaka Station|ja|阪急豊中駅}} to Ishibashi. This new route closely aligns with today’s National Route 176. Modern infrastructure, such as {{ill|Hankyu Electric Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}'s {{ill|Hankyu Takarazuka Line|ja|阪急宝塚本線}} and National Routes 176 and 173, run parallel to the old road. The {{ill|Nose Electric Railway|ja|能勢電鉄}}'s {{ill|Nose Electric Railway Myōken Line|ja|能勢電鉄妙見線}} also parallels the highway north of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyogo|ja|川西市}}. These developments led to the decline of the old highway, though much of its path remains as residential roads. Commercial hubs such as Okamachi and the area around {{ill|Ishibashi Handai-mae Station|ja|石橋阪大前駅}} have preserved the historical route as shopping streets. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')
({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == See Also == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Historic Roads]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Hyogo]] [[Category:Roads in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Roads in Hyogo Prefecture]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:suQqqq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:能勢街道]] [[Category:New]]

To the left is Harada Shrine.]] The '''Nose Kaidō''' (能勢街道) is an old highway stretching from {{ill|Nakatsu, Kita, Osaka|ja|中津 (大阪市)}} in [[Osaka]]'s Kita Ward, passing through {{ill|Ikeda, Osaka|ja|池田市}}, and ending at {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, where the {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} is located. From Chōkōji-minami, the highway splits, with the {{ill|Minoh Kaidō|ja|箕面街道}} providing a pilgrimage route to sites such as {{ill|Minoh Waterfall|ja|箕面の滝}}, {{ill|Ryuan-ji (Minoh)|ja|瀧安寺}}, and {{ill|Katsuo-ji|ja|勝尾寺}}. Today, the old road name is also used colloquially for the routes of {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} (Osaka–Toyonaka–Ikeda) and {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} (Ikeda–Tada–Nose–Sasayama), excluding bypasses. == History and Geography == The Nose Kaidō diverged from the {{ill|Chūgoku Kaidō|ja|中国街道}} in Nakatsu, then passed through {{ill|Jūso|ja|十三 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Mikuni, Osaka|ja|三国 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Shōnai, Toyonaka|ja|庄内 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Okamachi, Toyonaka|ja|岡町 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}}, Ikeda, Kibe, Furue, {{ill|Tada Station (Hyogo)|ja|多田駅 (兵庫県)}}, and {{ill|Ichinotorii Station|ja|一の鳥居駅}}, before following the ridgeline to Myōken Mountain. This highway facilitated the transportation of goods such as sake, textiles, and timber from Ikeda and Nose to Osaka. Additionally, rice, chestnuts, charcoal, silver, and copper from {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}} were carried along this route. Many significant religious sites lined the highway, including {{ill|Hattori Tenjingu Shrine|ja|服部天神宮}}, {{ill|Tōkō-in (Toyonaka)|ja|東光院 (豊中市)}} (Hagi Temple), {{ill|Harada Shrine (Toyonaka)|ja|原田神社}}, and {{ill|Tada Shrine|ja|多田神社}}. Other sites, such as {{ill|Nakayama-dera|ja|中山寺}} and Katsuo-ji, located off the main route, also attracted pilgrims. In 1876, the Nose Kaidō was designated as one of three first-class main roads in Osaka Prefecture under Daijōkan Order No. 60, which established road classifications. Historically, the route between Okamachi and Ikeda passed through Mount Tonoyama (south of Osaka University's Toyonaka Campus). Due to the steep incline, which hampered the transport of goods, a flatter "New Nose Kaidō" was built in 1894, running from the vicinity of present-day {{ill|Hankyu Toyonaka Station|ja|阪急豊中駅}} to Ishibashi. This new route closely aligns with today’s National Route 176. Modern infrastructure, such as {{ill|Hankyu Electric Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}'s {{ill|Hankyu Takarazuka Line|ja|阪急宝塚本線}} and National Routes 176 and 173, run parallel to the old road. The {{ill|Nose Electric Railway|ja|能勢電鉄}}'s {{ill|Nose Electric Railway Myōken Line|ja|能勢電鉄妙見線}} also parallels the highway north of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyogo|ja|川西市}}. These developments led to the decline of the old highway, though much of its path remains as residential roads. Commercial hubs such as Okamachi and the area around {{ill|Ishibashi Handai-mae Station|ja|石橋阪大前駅}} have preserved the historical route as shopping streets. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')
({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == See Also == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} [[Category:Historic Roads]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Hyogo]] [[Category:Roads in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Roads in Hyogo Prefecture]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:suQqqq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:能勢街道]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nose Highway]][[Category:qq]]

Due to the steep incline, which hampered the transport of goods, a flatter "New Nose Kaidō" was built in 1894, running from the vicinity of present-day {{ill|Hankyu Toyonaka Station|ja|阪急豊中駅}} to Ishibashi. This new route closely aligns with today’s National Route 176. Modern infrastructure, such as {{ill|Hankyu Electric Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}'s {{ill|Hankyu Takarazuka Line|ja|阪急宝塚本線}} and National Routes 176 and 173, run parallel to the old road. The {{ill|Nose Electric Railway|ja|能勢電鉄}}'s {{ill|Nose Electric Railway Myōken Line|ja|能勢電鉄妙見線}} also parallels the highway north of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyogo|ja|川西市}}. These developments led to the decline of the old highway, though much of its path remains as residential roads. Commercial hubs such as Okamachi and the area around {{ill|Ishibashi Handai-mae Station|ja|石橋阪大前駅}} have preserved the historical route as shopping streets. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')
({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == See Also == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} * {{ill|National Route 173 {{ill|Nose Highway|ja|能勢街道|en}} [[Image: Nose kaido&Harada shrine.jpg|thumbnail|240px|Nose Kaidō near Okamachi, which has become a shopping street. To the left is Harada Shrine.]] The '''Nose Kaidō''' (能勢街道) is an old highway stretching from {{ill|Nakatsu, Kita, Osaka|ja|中津 (大阪市)}} in [[Osaka]]'s Kita Ward, passing through {{ill|Ikeda, Osaka|ja|池田市}}, and ending at {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, where the {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} is located. From Chōkōji-minami, the highway splits, with the {{ill|Minoh Kaidō|ja|箕面街道}} providing a pilgrimage route to sites such as {{ill|Minoh Waterfall|ja|箕面の滝}}, {{ill|Ryuan-ji (Minoh)|ja|瀧安寺}}, and {{ill|Katsuo-ji|ja|勝尾寺}}. Today, the old road name is also used colloquially for the routes of {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} (Osaka–Toyonaka–Ikeda) and {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} (Ikeda–Tada–Nose–Sasayama), excluding bypasses. == History and Geography == The Nose Kaidō diverged from the {{ill|Chūgoku Kaidō|ja|中国街道}} in Nakatsu, then passed through {{ill|Jūso|ja|十三 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Mikuni, Osaka|ja|三国 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Shōnai, Toyonaka|ja|庄内 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Okamachi, Toyonaka|ja|岡町 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}}, Ikeda, Kibe, Furue, {{ill|Tada Station (Hyogo)|ja|多田駅 (兵庫県)}}, and {{ill|Ichinotorii Station|ja|一の鳥居駅}}, before following the ridgeline to Myōken Mountain. This highway facilitated the transportation of goods such as sake, textiles, and timber from Ikeda and Nose to Osaka. Additionally, rice, chestnuts, charcoal, silver, and copper from {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}} were carried along this route. Many significant religious sites lined the highway, including {{ill|Hattori Tenjingu Shrine|ja|服部天神宮}}, {{ill|Tōkō-in (Toyonaka)|ja|東光院 (豊中市)}} (Hagi Temple), {{ill|Harada Shrine (Toyonaka)|ja|原田神社}}, and {{ill|Tada Shrine|ja|多田神社}}. Other sites, such as {{ill|Nakayama-dera|ja|中山寺}} and Katsuo-ji, located off the main route, also attracted pilgrims. In 1876, the Nose Kaidō was designated as one of three first-class main roads in Osaka Prefecture under Daijōkan Order No. 60, which established road classifications. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')

Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == See Also == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/
