revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:Completion tier 1]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[Image: Nose kaido&Harada shrine.jpg|thumbnail|240px|Nose Kaidō near Okamachi, which has become a shopping street. To the left is Harada Shrine.]] The '''Nose Kaidō''' (能勢街道) is an old highway stretching from {{ill|Nakatsu, Kita, Osaka|ja|中津 (大阪市)}} in [[Osaka]]'s Kita Ward, passing through {{ill|Ikeda, Osaka|ja|池田市}}, and ending at {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, where the {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} is located. From Chōkōji-minami, the highway splits, with the {{ill|Minoh Kaidō|ja|箕面街道}} providing a pilgrimage route to sites such as {{ill|Minoh Waterfall|ja|箕面の滝}}, {{ill|Ryuan-ji (Minoh)|ja|瀧安寺}}, and {{ill|Katsuo-ji|ja|勝尾寺}}. Today, the old road name is also used colloquially for the routes of {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} (Osaka–Toyonaka–Ikeda) and {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} (Ikeda–Tada–Nose–Sasayama), excluding bypasses. == History and Geography == The Nose Kaidō diverged from the {{ill|Chūgoku Kaidō|ja|中国街道}} in Nakatsu, then passed through {{ill|Jūso|ja|十三 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Mikuni, Osaka|ja|三国 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Shōnai, Toyonaka|ja|庄内 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Okamachi, Toyonaka|ja|岡町 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}}, Ikeda, Kibe, Furue, {{ill|Tada Station (Hyogo)|ja|多田駅 (兵庫県)}}, and {{ill|Ichinotorii Station|ja|一の鳥居駅}}, before following the ridgeline to Myōken Mountain. This highway facilitated the transportation of goods such as sake, textiles, and timber from Ikeda and Nose to Osaka. Additionally, rice, chestnuts, charcoal, silver, and copper from {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}} were carried along this route. Many significant religious sites lined the highway, including {{ill|Hattori Tenjingu Shrine|ja|服部天神宮}}, {{ill|Tōkō-in (Toyonaka)|ja|東光院 (豊中市)}} (Hagi Temple), {{ill|Harada Shrine (Toyonaka)|ja|原田神社}}, and {{ill|Tada Shrine|ja|多田神社}}. Other sites, such as {{ill|Nakayama-dera|ja|中山寺}} and Katsuo-ji, located off the main route, also attracted pilgrims. In 1876, the Nose Kaidō was designated as one of three first-class main roads in Osaka Prefecture under Daijōkan Order No. 60, which established road classifications. Historically, the route between Okamachi and Ikeda passed through Mount Tonoyama (south of Osaka University's Toyonaka Campus). Due to the steep incline, which hampered the transport of goods, a flatter "New Nose Kaidō" was built in 1894, running from the vicinity of present-day {{ill|Hankyu Toyonaka Station|ja|阪急豊中駅}} to Ishibashi. This new route closely aligns with today’s National Route 176. Modern infrastructure, such as {{ill|Hankyu Electric Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}'s {{ill|Hankyu Takarazuka Line|ja|阪急宝塚本線}} and National Routes 176 and 173, run parallel to the old road. The {{ill|Nose Electric Railway|ja|能勢電鉄}}'s {{ill|Nose Electric Railway Myōken Line|ja|能勢電鉄妙見線}} also parallels the highway north of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyogo|ja|川西市}}. These developments led to the decline of the old highway, though much of its path remains as residential roads. Commercial hubs such as Okamachi and the area around {{ill|Ishibashi Handai-mae Station|ja|石橋阪大前駅}} have preserved the historical route as shopping streets. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')
({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == See Also == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} [[Image: Nose kaido&Harada shrine.jpg|thumbnail|240px|Nose Kaidō near Okamachi, which has become a shopping street. To the left is Harada Shrine.]] The '''Nose Kaidō''' (能勢街道) is an old highway stretching from {{ill|Nakatsu, Kita, Osaka|ja|中津 (大阪市)}} in [[Osaka]]'s Kita Ward, passing through {{ill|Ikeda, Osaka|ja|池田市}}, and ending at {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, where the {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)}} is located. From Chōkōji-minami, the highway splits, with the {{ill|Minoh Kaidō|ja|箕面街道}} providing a pilgrimage route to sites such as {{ill|Minoh Waterfall|ja|箕面の滝}}, {{ill|Ryuan-ji (Minoh)|ja|瀧安寺}}, and {{ill|Katsuo-ji|ja|勝尾寺}}. Today, the old road name is also used colloquially for the routes of {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} (Osaka–Toyonaka–Ikeda) and {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} (Ikeda–Tada–Nose–Sasayama), excluding bypasses. == History and Geography == The Nose Kaidō diverged from the {{ill|Chūgoku Kaidō|ja|中国街道}} in Nakatsu, then passed through {{ill|Jūso|ja|十三 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Mikuni, Osaka|ja|三国 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Shōnai, Toyonaka|ja|庄内 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Okamachi, Toyonaka|ja|岡町 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}}, Ikeda, Kibe, Furue, {{ill|Tada Station (Hyogo)|ja|多田駅 (兵庫県)}}, and {{ill|Ichinotorii Station|ja|一の鳥居駅}}, before following the ridgeline to Myōken Mountain. This highway facilitated the transportation of goods such as sake, textiles, and timber from Ikeda and Nose to Osaka. Additionally, rice, chestnuts, charcoal, silver, and copper from {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}} were carried along this route. Many significant religious sites lined the highway, including {{ill|Hattori Tenjingu Shrine|ja|服部天神宮}}, {{ill|Tōkō-in (Toyonaka)|ja|東光院 (豊中市)}} (Hagi Temple), {{ill|Harada Shrine (Toyonaka)|ja|原田神社}}, and {{ill|Tada Shrine|ja|多田神社}}. Other sites, such as {{ill|Nakayama-dera|ja|中山寺}} and Katsuo-ji, located off the main route, also attracted pilgrims. In 1876, the Nose Kaidō was designated as one of three first-class main roads in Osaka Prefecture under Daijōkan Order No. 60, which established road classifications. Historically, the route between Okamachi and Ikeda passed through Mount Tonoyama (south of Osaka University's Toyonaka Campus). Due to the steep incline, which hampered the transport of goods, a flatter "New Nose Kaidō" was built in 1894, running from the vicinity of present-day {{ill|Hankyu Toyonaka Station|ja|阪急豊中駅}} to Ishibashi. This new route closely aligns with today’s National Route 176. Modern infrastructure, such as {{ill|Hankyu Electric Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}'s {{ill|Hankyu Takarazuka Line|ja|阪急宝塚本線}} and National Routes 176 and 173, run parallel to the old road. The {{ill|Nose Electric Railway|ja|能勢電鉄}}'s {{ill|Nose Electric Railway Myōken Line|ja|能勢電鉄妙見線}} also parallels the highway north of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyogo|ja|川西市}}. These developments led to the decline of the old highway, though much of its path remains as residential roads. Commercial hubs such as Okamachi and the area around {{ill|Ishibashi Handai-mae Station|ja|石橋阪大前駅}} have preserved the historical route as shopping streets. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')
({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == See Also == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} [[Image: Nose kaido&Harada shrine.jpg|thumbnail|240px|Nose Kaidō near Okamachi, which has become a shopping street. To the left is Harada Shrine.]] The '''Nose Kaidō''' (能勢街道) is an old highway stretching from {{ill|Nakatsu, Kita, Osaka|ja|中津 (大阪市)}} in [[Osaka]]'s Kita Ward, passing through {{ill|Ikeda, Osaka|ja|池田市}}, and ending at {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, where the {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)}} is located. From Chōkōji-minami, the highway splits, with the {{ill|Minoh Kaidō|ja|箕面街道}} providing a pilgrimage route to sites such as {{ill|Minoh Waterfall|ja|箕面の滝}}, {{ill|Ryuan-ji (Minoh)|ja|瀧安寺}}, and {{ill|Katsuo-ji|ja|勝尾寺}}. Today, the old road name is also used colloquially for the routes of {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} (Osaka–Toyonaka–Ikeda) and {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} (Ikeda–Tada–Nose–Sasayama), excluding bypasses. == History and Geography == The Nose Kaidō diverged from the {{ill|Chūgoku Kaidō|ja|中国街道}} in Nakatsu, then passed through {{ill|Jūso|ja|十三 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Mikuni, Osaka|ja|三国 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Shōnai, Toyonaka|ja|庄内 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Okamachi, Toyonaka|ja|岡町 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}}, Ikeda, Kibe, Furue, {{ill|Tada Station (Hyogo)|ja|多田駅 (兵庫県)}}, and {{ill|Ichinotorii Station|ja|一の鳥居駅}}, before following the ridgeline to Myōken Mountain. This highway facilitated the transportation of goods such as sake, textiles, and timber from Ikeda and Nose to Osaka. Additionally, rice, chestnuts, charcoal, silver, and copper from {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}} were carried along this route. Many significant religious sites lined the highway, including {{ill|Hattori Tenjingu Shrine|ja|服部天神宮}}, {{ill|Tōkō-in (Toyonaka)|ja|東光院 (豊中市)}} (Hagi Temple), {{ill|Harada Shrine (Toyonaka)|ja|原田神社}}, and {{ill|Tada Shrine|ja|多田神社}}. Other sites, such as {{ill|Nakayama-dera|ja|中山寺}} and Katsuo-ji, located off the main route, also attracted pilgrims. In 1876, the Nose Kaidō was designated as one of three first-class main roads in Osaka Prefecture under Daijōkan Order No. 60, which established road classifications. Historically, the route between Okamachi and Ikeda passed through Mount Tonoyama (south of Osaka University's Toyonaka Campus). Due to the steep incline, which hampered the transport of goods, a flatter "New Nose Kaidō" was built in 1894, running from the vicinity of present-day {{ill|Hankyu Toyonaka Station|ja|阪急豊中駅}} to Ishibashi. This new route closely aligns with today’s National Route 176. Modern infrastructure, such as {{ill|Hankyu Electric Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}'s {{ill|Hankyu Takarazuka Line|ja|阪急宝塚本線}} and National Routes 176 and 173, run parallel to the old road. The {{ill|Nose Electric Railway|ja|能勢電鉄}}'s {{ill|Nose Electric Railway Myōken Line|ja|能勢電鉄妙見線}} also parallels the highway north of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyogo|ja|川西市}}. These developments led to the decline of the old highway, though much of its path remains as residential roads. Commercial hubs such as Okamachi and the area around {{ill|Ishibashi Handai-mae Station|ja|石橋阪大前駅}} have preserved the historical route as shopping streets. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')
({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == See Also == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} To the left is Harada Shrine.]] The '''Nose Kaidō''' (能勢街道) is an old highway stretching from {{ill|Nakatsu, Kita, Osaka|ja|中津 (大阪市)}} in [[Osaka]]'s Kita Ward, passing through {{ill|Ikeda, Osaka|ja|池田市}}, and ending at {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, where the {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)}} is located. From Chōkōji-minami, the highway splits, with the {{ill|Minoh Kaidō|ja|箕面街道}} providing a pilgrimage route to sites such as {{ill|Minoh Waterfall|ja|箕面の滝}}, {{ill|Ryuan-ji (Minoh)|ja|瀧安寺}}, and {{ill|Katsuo-ji|ja|勝尾寺}}. Today, the old road name is also used colloquially for the routes of {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} (Osaka–Toyonaka–Ikeda) and {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} (Ikeda–Tada–Nose–Sasayama), excluding bypasses. == History and Geography == The Nose Kaidō diverged from the {{ill|Chūgoku Kaidō|ja|中国街道}} in Nakatsu, then passed through {{ill|Jūso|ja|十三 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Mikuni, Osaka|ja|三国 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Shōnai, Toyonaka|ja|庄内 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Okamachi, Toyonaka|ja|岡町 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}}, Ikeda, Kibe, Furue, {{ill|Tada Station (Hyogo)|ja|多田駅 (兵庫県)}}, and {{ill|Ichinotorii Station|ja|一の鳥居駅}}, before following the ridgeline to Myōken Mountain. This highway facilitated the transportation of goods such as sake, textiles, and timber from Ikeda and Nose to Osaka. Additionally, rice, chestnuts, charcoal, silver, and copper from {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}} were carried along this route. Many significant religious sites lined the highway, including {{ill|Hattori Tenjingu Shrine|ja|服部天神宮}}, {{ill|Tōkō-in (Toyonaka)|ja|東光院 (豊中市)}} (Hagi Temple), {{ill|Harada Shrine (Toyonaka)|ja|原田神社}}, and {{ill|Tada Shrine|ja|多田神社}}. Other sites, such as {{ill|Nakayama-dera|ja|中山寺}} and Katsuo-ji, located off the main route, also attracted pilgrims. In 1876, the Nose Kaidō was designated as one of three first-class main roads in Osaka Prefecture under Daijōkan Order No. 60, which established road classifications. Historically, the route between Okamachi and Ikeda passed through Mount Tonoyama (south of Osaka University's Toyonaka Campus). Due to the steep incline, which hampered the transport of goods, a flatter "New Nose Kaidō" was built in 1894, running from the vicinity of present-day {{ill|Hankyu Toyonaka Station|ja|阪急豊中駅}} to Ishibashi. This new route closely aligns with today’s National Route 176. Modern infrastructure, such as {{ill|Hankyu Electric Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}'s {{ill|Hankyu Takarazuka Line|ja|阪急宝塚本線}} and National Routes 176 and 173, run parallel to the old road. The {{ill|Nose Electric Railway|ja|能勢電鉄}}'s {{ill|Nose Electric Railway Myōken Line|ja|能勢電鉄妙見線}} also parallels the highway north of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyogo|ja|川西市}}. These developments led to the decline of the old highway, though much of its path remains as residential roads. Commercial hubs such as Okamachi and the area around {{ill|Ishibashi Handai-mae Station|ja|石橋阪大前駅}} have preserved the historical route as shopping streets. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')
({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty.== See Also == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} To the left is Harada Shrine.]] The '''Nose Kaidō''' (能勢街道) is an old highway stretching from {{ill|Nakatsu, Kita, Osaka|ja|中津 (大阪市)}} in [[Osaka]]'s Kita Ward, passing through {{ill|Ikeda, Osaka|ja|池田市}}, and ending at {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, where the {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)}} is located. From Chōkōji-minami, the highway splits, with the {{ill|Minoh Kaidō|ja|箕面街道}} providing a pilgrimage route to sites such as {{ill|Minoh Waterfall|ja|箕面の滝}}, {{ill|Ryuan-ji (Minoh)|ja|瀧安寺}}, and {{ill|Katsuo-ji|ja|勝尾寺}}. Today, the old road name is also used colloquially for the routes of {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} (Osaka–Toyonaka–Ikeda) and {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} (Ikeda–Tada–Nose–Sasayama), excluding bypasses. == History and Geography == The Nose Kaidō diverged from the {{ill|Chūgoku Kaidō|ja|中国街道}} in Nakatsu, then passed through {{ill|Jūso|ja|十三 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Mikuni, Osaka|ja|三国 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Shōnai, Toyonaka|ja|庄内 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Okamachi, Toyonaka|ja|岡町 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}}, Ikeda, Kibe, Furue, {{ill|Tada Station (Hyogo)|ja|多田駅 (兵庫県)}}, and {{ill|Ichinotorii Station|ja|一の鳥居駅}}, before following the ridgeline to Myōken Mountain. This highway facilitated the transportation of goods such as sake, textiles, and timber from Ikeda and Nose to Osaka. Additionally, rice, chestnuts, charcoal, silver, and copper from {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}} were carried along this route. Many significant religious sites lined the highway, including {{ill|Hattori Tenjingu Shrine|ja|服部天神宮}}, {{ill|Tōkō-in (Toyonaka)|ja|東光院 (豊中市)}} (Hagi Temple), {{ill|Harada Shrine (Toyonaka)|ja|原田神社}}, and {{ill|Tada Shrine|ja|多田神社}}. Other sites, such as {{ill|Nakayama-dera|ja|中山寺}} and Katsuo-ji, located off the main route, also attracted pilgrims. In 1876, the Nose Kaidō was designated as one of three first-class main roads in Osaka Prefecture under Daijōkan Order No. 60, which established road classifications. Historically, the route between Okamachi and Ikeda passed through Mount Tonoyama (south of Osaka University's Toyonaka Campus). Due to the steep incline, which hampered the transport of goods, a flatter "New Nose Kaidō" was built in 1894, running from the vicinity of present-day {{ill|Hankyu Toyonaka Station|ja|阪急豊中駅}} to Ishibashi. This new route closely aligns with today’s National Route 176. Modern infrastructure, such as {{ill|Hankyu Electric Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}'s {{ill|Hankyu Takarazuka Line|ja|阪急宝塚本線}} and National Routes 176 and 173, run parallel to the old road. The {{ill|Nose Electric Railway|ja|能勢電鉄}}'s {{ill|Nose Electric Railway Myōken Line|ja|能勢電鉄妙見線}} also parallels the highway north of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyogo|ja|川西市}}. These developments led to the decline of the old highway, though much of its path remains as residential roads. Commercial hubs such as Okamachi and the area around {{ill|Ishibashi Handai-mae Station|ja|石橋阪大前駅}} have preserved the historical route as shopping streets. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')
({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == See Also == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} To the left is Harada Shrine.]] The '''Nose Kaidō''' (能勢街道) is an old highway stretching from {{ill|Nakatsu, Kita, Osaka|ja|中津 (大阪市)}} in [[Osaka]]'s Kita Ward, passing through {{ill|Ikeda, Osaka|ja|池田市}}, and ending at {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, where the {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)}} is located. From Chōkōji-minami, the highway splits, with the {{ill|Minoh Kaidō|ja|箕面街道}} providing a pilgrimage route to sites such as {{ill|Minoh Waterfall|ja|箕面の滝}}, {{ill|Ryuan-ji (Minoh)|ja|瀧安寺}}, and {{ill|Katsuo-ji|ja|勝尾寺}}. Today, the old road name is also used colloquially for the routes of {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} (Osaka–Toyonaka–Ikeda) and {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} (Ikeda–Tada–Nose–Sasayama), excluding bypasses. == History and Geography == The Nose Kaidō diverged from the {{ill|Chūgoku Kaidō|ja|中国街道}} in Nakatsu, then passed through {{ill|Jūso|ja|十三 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Mikuni, Osaka|ja|三国 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Shōnai, Toyonaka|ja|庄内 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Okamachi, Toyonaka|ja|岡町 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}}, Ikeda, Kibe, Furue, {{ill|Tada Station (Hyogo)|ja|多田駅 (兵庫県)}}, and {{ill|Ichinotorii Station|ja|一の鳥居駅}}, before following the ridgeline to Myōken Mountain. This highway facilitated the transportation of goods such as sake, textiles, and timber from Ikeda and Nose to Osaka. Additionally, rice, chestnuts, charcoal, silver, and copper from {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}} were carried along this route. Many significant religious sites lined the highway, including {{ill|Hattori Tenjingu Shrine|ja|服部天神宮}}, {{ill|Tōkō-in (Toyonaka)|ja|東光院 (豊中市)}} (Hagi Temple), {{ill|Harada Shrine (Toyonaka)|ja|原田神社}}, and {{ill|Tada Shrine|ja|多田神社}}. Other sites, such as {{ill|Nakayama-dera|ja|中山寺}} and Katsuo-ji, located off the main route, also attracted pilgrims. In 1876, the Nose Kaidō was designated as one of three first-class main roads in Osaka Prefecture under Daijōkan Order No. 60, which established road classifications. Historically, the route between Okamachi and Ikeda passed through Mount Tonoyama (south of Osaka University's Toyonaka Campus). Due to the steep incline, which hampered the transport of goods, a flatter "New Nose Kaidō" was built in 1894, running from the vicinity of present-day {{ill|Hankyu Toyonaka Station|ja|阪急豊中駅}} to Ishibashi. This new route closely aligns with today’s National Route 176. Modern infrastructure, such as {{ill|Hankyu Electric Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}'s {{ill|Hankyu Takarazuka Line|ja|阪急宝塚本線}} and National Routes 176 and 173, run parallel to the old road. The {{ill|Nose Electric Railway|ja|能勢電鉄}}'s {{ill|Nose Electric Railway Myōken Line|ja|能勢電鉄妙見線}} also parallels the highway north of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyogo|ja|川西市}}. These developments led to the decline of the old highway, though much of its path remains as residential roads. Commercial hubs such as Okamachi and the area around {{ill|Ishibashi Handai-mae Station|ja|石橋阪大前駅}} have preserved the historical route as shopping streets. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')
({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == See Also == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} Many significant religious sites lined the highway, including {{ill|Hattori Tenjingu Shrine|ja|服部天神宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tōkō-in (Toyonaka)|ja|東光院 (豊中市)|12=draft}} (Hagi Temple), {{ill|Harada Shrine (Toyonaka)|ja|原田神社|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tada Shrine|ja|多田神社|12=draft}}. Other sites, such as {{ill|Nakayama-dera|ja|中山寺|12=draft}} and Katsuo-ji, located off the main route, also attracted pilgrims. In 1876, the Nose Kaidō was designated as one of three first-class main roads in Osaka Prefecture under Daijōkan Order No. 60, which established road classifications. Historically, the route between Okamachi and Ikeda passed through Mount Tonoyama (south of Osaka University's Toyonaka Campus). Due to the steep incline, which hampered the transport of goods, a flatter "New Nose Kaidō" was built in 1894, running from the vicinity of present-day {{ill|Hankyu Toyonaka Station|ja|阪急豊中駅|12=draft}} to Ishibashi. This new route closely aligns with today’s National Route 176. Modern infrastructure, such as {{ill|Hankyu Electric Railway|ja|阪急電鉄|12=draft}}'s {{ill|Hankyu Takarazuka Line|ja|阪急宝塚本線|12=draft}} and National Routes 176 and 173, run parallel to the old road. The {{ill|Nose Electric Railway|ja|能勢電鉄|12=draft}}'s {{ill|Nose Electric Railway Myōken Line|ja|能勢電鉄妙見線|12=draft}} also parallels the highway north of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyogo|ja|川西市|12=draft}}. These developments led to the decline of the old highway, though much of its path remains as residential roads. Commercial hubs such as Okamachi and the area around {{ill|Ishibashi Handai-mae Station|ja|石橋阪大前駅|12=draft}} have preserved the historical route as shopping streets. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災|12=draft}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)|12=draft}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道|12=draft}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い|12=draft}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')
({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭|12=draft}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休|12=draft}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春|12=draft}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == See Also == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号|12=draft}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)|12=draft}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Historic Roads]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Hyogo]] [[Category:Roads in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Roads in Hyogo Prefecture]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[sn:Nose Highway]] [[ja:能勢街道]] [[en:Nose Highway]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Nose Highway}}

[[Image: Nose kaido&Harada shrine.jpg|thumbnail|240px|Nose Kaidō near Okamachi, which has become a shopping street. To the left is Harada Shrine.]] The '''Nose Kaidō''' (能勢街道) is an old highway stretching from {{ill|Nakatsu, Kita, Osaka|ja|中津 (大阪市)|12=draft}} in [[Osaka]]'s Kita Ward, passing through {{ill|Ikeda, Osaka|ja|池田市|12=draft}}, and ending at {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)|12=draft}}, where the {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)|12=draft}} is located. From Chōkōji-minami, the highway splits, with the {{ill|Minoh Kaidō|ja|箕面街道|12=draft}} providing a pilgrimage route to sites such as {{ill|Minoh Waterfall|ja|箕面の滝|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ryuan-ji (Minoh)|ja|瀧安寺|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Katsuo-ji|ja|勝尾寺|12=draft}}. Today, the old road name is also used colloquially for the routes of {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号|12=draft}} (Osaka–Toyonaka–Ikeda) and {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号|12=draft}} (Ikeda–Tada–Nose–Sasayama), excluding bypasses. == History and Geography == The Nose Kaidō diverged from the {{ill|Chūgoku Kaidō|ja|中国街道|12=draft}} in Nakatsu, then passed through {{ill|Jūso|ja|十三 (大阪市)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mikuni, Osaka|ja|三国 (大阪市)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shōnai, Toyonaka|ja|庄内 (豊中市)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Okamachi, Toyonaka|ja|岡町 (豊中市)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)|12=draft}}, Ikeda, Kibe, Furue, {{ill|Tada Station (Hyogo)|ja|多田駅 (兵庫県)|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Ichinotorii Station|ja|一の鳥居駅|12=draft}}, before following the ridgeline to Myōken Mountain. The {{ill|Nose Electric Railway|ja|能勢電鉄|12=draft}}'s {{ill|Nose Electric Railway Myōken Line|ja|能勢電鉄妙見線|12=draft}} also parallels the highway north of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyogo|ja|川西市|12=draft}}. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災|12=draft}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)|12=draft}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道|12=draft}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い|12=draft}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')
({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭|12=draft}}" for tea ceremonies. ({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭|12=draft}}" for tea ceremonies. Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == See Also == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号|12=draft}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)|12=draft}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Historic Roads]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Hyogo]] [[Category:Roads in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Roads in Hyogo Prefecture]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:return the see also]] }} [[sn:Nose Highway]] [[ja:能勢街道]] [[en:Nose Highway]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Nose Highway}}

revision.text:
[[Image: Nose kaido&Harada shrine.jpg|thumbnail|240px|Nose Kaidō near Okamachi, which has become a shopping street. To the left is Harada Shrine.]] The '''Nose Kaidō''' (能勢街道) is an old highway stretching from {{ill|Nakatsu, Kita, Osaka|ja|中津 (大阪市)|12=draft}} in [[Osaka]]'s Kita Ward, passing through {{ill|Ikeda, Osaka|ja|池田市|12=draft}}, and ending at {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)|12=draft}}, where the {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)|12=draft}} is located. From Chōkōji-minami, the highway splits, with the {{ill|Minoh Kaidō|ja|箕面街道|12=draft}} providing a pilgrimage route to sites such as {{ill|Minoh Waterfall|ja|箕面の滝|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ryuan-ji (Minoh)|ja|瀧安寺|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Katsuo-ji|ja|勝尾寺|12=draft}}. Today, the old road name is also used colloquially for the routes of {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号|12=draft}} (Osaka–Toyonaka–Ikeda) and {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号|12=draft}} (Ikeda–Tada–Nose–Sasayama), excluding bypasses. == History and Geography == The Nose Kaidō diverged from the {{ill|Chūgoku Kaidō|ja|中国街道|12=draft}} in Nakatsu, then passed through {{ill|Jūso|ja|十三 (大阪市)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mikuni, Osaka|ja|三国 (大阪市)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shōnai, Toyonaka|ja|庄内 (豊中市)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Okamachi, Toyonaka|ja|岡町 (豊中市)|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)|12=draft}}, Ikeda, Kibe, Furue, {{ill|Tada Station (Hyogo)|ja|多田駅 (兵庫県)|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Ichinotorii Station|ja|一の鳥居駅|12=draft}}, before following the ridgeline to Myōken Mountain. This highway facilitated the transportation of goods such as sake, textiles, and timber from Ikeda and Nose to Osaka. Additionally, rice, chestnuts, charcoal, silver, and copper from {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国|12=draft}} were carried along this route. Many significant religious sites lined the highway, including {{ill|Hattori Tenjingu Shrine|ja|服部天神宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tōkō-in (Toyonaka)|ja|東光院 (豊中市)|12=draft}} (Hagi Temple), {{ill|Harada Shrine (Toyonaka)|ja|原田神社|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tada Shrine|ja|多田神社|12=draft}}. Other sites, such as {{ill|Nakayama-dera|ja|中山寺|12=draft}} and Katsuo-ji, located off the main route, also attracted pilgrims. In 1876, the Nose Kaidō was designated as one of three first-class main roads in Osaka Prefecture under Daijōkan Order No. 60, which established road classifications. Historically, the route between Okamachi and Ikeda passed through Mount Tonoyama (south of Osaka University's Toyonaka Campus). Due to the steep incline, which hampered the transport of goods, a flatter "New Nose Kaidō" was built in 1894, running from the vicinity of present-day {{ill|Hankyu Toyonaka Station|ja|阪急豊中駅|12=draft}} to Ishibashi. This new route closely aligns with today’s National Route 176. Modern infrastructure, such as {{ill|Hankyu Electric Railway|ja|阪急電鉄|12=draft}}'s {{ill|Hankyu Takarazuka Line|ja|阪急宝塚本線|12=draft}} and National Routes 176 and 173, run parallel to the old road. The {{ill|Nose Electric Railway|ja|能勢電鉄|12=draft}}'s {{ill|Nose Electric Railway Myōken Line|ja|能勢電鉄妙見線|12=draft}} also parallels the highway north of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyogo|ja|川西市|12=draft}}. These developments led to the decline of the old highway, though much of its path remains as residential roads. Commercial hubs such as Okamachi and the area around {{ill|Ishibashi Handai-mae Station|ja|石橋阪大前駅|12=draft}} have preserved the historical route as shopping streets. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災|12=draft}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)|12=draft}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道|12=draft}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い|12=draft}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')
({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭|12=draft}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休|12=draft}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春|12=draft}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == See Also == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号|12=draft}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)|12=draft}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Historic Roads]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Hyogo]] [[Category:Roads in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Roads in Hyogo Prefecture]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Nose Highway]] [[ja:能勢街道]] [[en:Nose Highway]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Nose Highway}}

[[Image: Nose kaido&Harada shrine.jpg|thumbnail|240px|Nose Kaidō near Okamachi, which has become a shopping street. To the left is Harada Shrine.]] The '''Nose Kaidō''' (能勢街道) is an old highway stretching from {{ill|Nakatsu, Kita, Osaka|ja|中津 (大阪市)|12=draft|12=draft}} in [[Osaka]]'s Kita Ward, passing through {{ill|Ikeda, Osaka|ja|池田市|12=draft|12=draft}}, and ending at {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)|12=draft|12=draft}}, where the {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)|12=draft|12=draft}} is located. From Chōkōji-minami, the highway splits, with the {{ill|Minoh Kaidō|ja|箕面街道|12=draft|12=draft}} providing a pilgrimage route to sites such as {{ill|Minoh Waterfall|ja|箕面の滝|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ryuan-ji (Minoh)|ja|瀧安寺|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Katsuo-ji|ja|勝尾寺|12=draft|12=draft}}. Today, the old road name is also used colloquially for the routes of {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号|12=draft|12=draft}} (Osaka–Toyonaka–Ikeda) and {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号|12=draft|12=draft}} (Ikeda–Tada–Nose–Sasayama), excluding bypasses. == History and Geography == The Nose Kaidō diverged from the {{ill|Chūgoku Kaidō|ja|中国街道|12=draft|12=draft}} in Nakatsu, then passed through {{ill|Jūso|ja|十三 (大阪市)|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Mikuni, Osaka|ja|三国 (大阪市)|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shōnai, Toyonaka|ja|庄内 (豊中市)|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Okamachi, Toyonaka|ja|岡町 (豊中市)|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)|12=draft|12=draft}}, Ikeda, Kibe, Furue, {{ill|Tada Station (Hyogo)|ja|多田駅 (兵庫県)|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Ichinotorii Station|ja|一の鳥居駅|12=draft|12=draft}}, before following the ridgeline to Myōken Mountain. This highway facilitated the transportation of goods such as sake, textiles, and timber from Ikeda and Nose to Osaka. Additionally, rice, chestnuts, charcoal, silver, and copper from {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国|12=draft|12=draft}} were carried along this route. Many significant religious sites lined the highway, including {{ill|Hattori Tenjingu Shrine|ja|服部天神宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tōkō-in (Toyonaka)|ja|東光院 (豊中市)|12=draft|12=draft}} (Hagi Temple), {{ill|Harada Shrine (Toyonaka)|ja|原田神社|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Tada Shrine|ja|多田神社|12=draft|12=draft}}. Other sites, such as {{ill|Nakayama-dera|ja|中山寺|12=draft|12=draft}} and Katsuo-ji, located off the main route, also attracted pilgrims. In 1876, the Nose Kaidō was designated as one of three first-class main roads in Osaka Prefecture under Daijōkan Order No. 60, which established road classifications. Historically, the route between Okamachi and Ikeda passed through Mount Tonoyama (south of Osaka University's Toyonaka Campus). Due to the steep incline, which hampered the transport of goods, a flatter "New Nose Kaidō" was built in 1894, running from the vicinity of present-day {{ill|Hankyu Toyonaka Station|ja|阪急豊中駅|12=draft|12=draft}} to Ishibashi. This new route closely aligns with today’s National Route 176. Modern infrastructure, such as {{ill|Hankyu Electric Railway|ja|阪急電鉄|12=draft|12=draft}}'s {{ill|Hankyu Takarazuka Line|ja|阪急宝塚本線|12=draft|12=draft}} and National Routes 176 and 173, run parallel to the old road. The {{ill|Nose Electric Railway|ja|能勢電鉄|12=draft|12=draft}}'s {{ill|Nose Electric Railway Myōken Line|ja|能勢電鉄妙見線|12=draft|12=draft}} also parallels the highway north of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyogo|ja|川西市|12=draft|12=draft}}. These developments led to the decline of the old highway, though much of its path remains as residential roads. Commercial hubs such as Okamachi and the area around {{ill|Ishibashi Handai-mae Station|ja|石橋阪大前駅|12=draft|12=draft}} have preserved the historical route as shopping streets. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災|12=draft|12=draft}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)|12=draft|12=draft}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道|12=draft|12=draft}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い|12=draft|12=draft}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')
({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭|12=draft|12=draft}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休|12=draft|12=draft}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春|12=draft|12=draft}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == See Also == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)|12=draft|12=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Historic Roads]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Hyogo]] [[Category:Roads in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Roads in Hyogo Prefecture]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Nose Highway]] [[ja:能勢街道]] [[en:Nose Highway]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Nose Highway}}

[[Image: Nose kaido&Harada shrine.jpg|thumbnail|240px|Nose Kaidō near Okamachi, which has become a shopping street. To the left is Harada Shrine.]] The '''Nose Kaidō''' (能勢街道) is an old highway stretching from {{ill|Nakatsu, Kita, Osaka|ja|中津 (大阪市)}} in [[Osaka]]'s Kita Ward, passing through {{ill|Ikeda, Osaka|ja|池田市}}, and ending at {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, where the {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)}} is located. revision.comment: 42 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:能勢街道 up to rev 102374857
revision.text:
[[画像: Nose kaido&Harada shrine.jpg|thumbnail|240px|能勢街道が商店街となった岡町付近
左手は原田神社]] '''能勢街道'''（のせかいどう）は、[[大阪府]][[大阪市]][[北区 (大阪市)|北区]][[中津 (大阪市)|中津]]1丁目から[[池田市]]を経て[[妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)|妙見山]]の[[能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)|能勢妙見堂]]に至る旧街道。能勢街道は長興寺南から分岐し、[[箕面の滝]]や[[瀧安寺]]、[[勝尾寺]]への参拝路の[[箕面街道]]がある。また、現在では旧街道に並行する、[[国道176号]]大阪 - 豊中 - 池田および[[国道173号]]池田 - 多田 - 能勢 - 篠山（ただしバイパス等を除く）の通称・別称として使われることも多い。 ==歴史と地理== 中津で[[中国街道]]から分岐して、[[十三 (大阪市)|十三]]、[[三国 (大阪市)|三国]]、[[庄内 (豊中市)|庄内]]、[[服部 (豊中市)|服部]]、[[岡町 (豊中市)|岡町]]、[[石橋 (大阪府)|石橋]]、池田、木部、古江、[[多田駅 (兵庫県)|多田]]、[[一の鳥居駅|一の鳥居]]、そして尾根筋を通り妙見山へ至る。 池田や[[能勢郡|能勢]]で産する酒や衣類、木材が当街道によって大坂へ運ばれ、さらには能勢から奥に続く[[丹波国]]の[[米]]、[[クリ|栗]]、[[炭]]、[[銀]]、[[銅]]などの搬出路でもあった。また終着地の能勢妙見をはじめ、沿道には[[服部天神宮]]、[[東光院 (豊中市)|東光院]]（萩の寺）、[[原田神社]]、[[多田神社]]など社寺が並び、街道の途中から入った[[中山寺 (宝塚市)|中山寺]]、[[勝尾寺]]などを含めての参拝路としても賑わいを見せた。明治9年（[[1876年]]）に太政官達60号で道路の種類および等級が定められた際、能勢街道は大阪府下で3本のみの一等縦貫線に掲げられている。 ところで岡町から池田まで、古くは刀根山（現在の[[大阪大学]]豊中キャンパス南方）回りの山道を通っていた。この坂が急で物資運搬に支障をきたすため、明治27年（[[1894年]]）に現在の阪急[[豊中駅]]付近から石橋にかけ、比較的平坦な新道が開かれた。これを「新能勢街道」といい、現在の[[国道176号]]ルートとほぼ同じである。 現在は大阪市から[[池田市]]まで[[阪急電鉄]][[阪急宝塚本線|宝塚線]]と国道176号が、池田市から妙見山麓まで[[国道173号]]および[[国道477号]]が並行している。また[[川西市]]以北では、[[能勢電鉄]][[能勢電鉄妙見線|妙見線]]が並行する。これら鉄道や道路の整備によって当街道は衰退したが、現在もかつての道筋の多くが残り、生活道路として利用されている。旧伊丹街道と交差する岡町付近や阪急電鉄宝塚線[[石橋阪大前駅]]周辺では商店街となっている。 [[阪神淡路大震災]]により、街道沿いの古い木造家屋は大きな打撃を受け、当時の街道を偲ぶ貴重な家屋も現在は建て替わったり駐車場などになり、かなり少なくなってしまった。 == 宿場一覧 == *[[服部 (豊中市)|服部]] *[[石橋 (大阪府)|石橋]]（瀬川宿） [[西国街道]]との結節点。 *池田 == 能勢街道を舞台にした作品 == *[[上方落語]]「[[池田の猪買い]]」（{{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=参照 |date=20011218151505}}） == 能勢街道沿道の名産 == *[[池田炭]] 切り口が菊のように美しいため「[[菊炭]]」と呼ばれているが池田がかつて集積場だったため「池田炭」とも呼ばれている。この炭は茶道用に使用される高級炭でかつては[[千利休]]も愛用したと言われる。 *[[日本酒|清酒]] 池田市の「[[呉春]]」は有名。 *[[猪肉]] かつて池田は猪の肉が名物であった。上方落語の「池田の猪買い」は大阪から池田に猪の買い入れにくる情景を描く。現在の北摂地域でもイノシシは生息するが、特産としての地位は失われた。 == 関連項目 == *[[国道176号]] *[[国道173号]] *[[妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)]] *[[能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)]] ==外部リンク== *[http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ 服部天神宮] *[http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 近畿道ものがたり] ラジオ大阪サイト。 *[https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html 旧能勢街道岡町界隈]豊中市サイト {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{デフォルトソート:のせかいとう}} [[Category:街道]] [[Category:大阪府の交通史]] [[Category:兵庫県の交通史]] [[Category:大阪府の道路]] [[Category:兵庫県の道路]]

[[画像: Nose kaido&Harada shrine.jpg|thumbnail|240px|能勢街道が商店街となった岡町付近
左手は原田神社]] '''能勢街道'''（のせかいどう）は、[[大阪府]][[大阪市]][[北区 (大阪市)|北区]][[中津 (大阪市)|中津]]1丁目から[[池田市]]を経て[[妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)|妙見山]]の[[能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)|能勢妙見堂]]に至る旧街道。能勢街道は長興寺南から分岐し、[[箕面の滝]]や[[瀧安寺]]、[[勝尾寺]]への参拝路の[[箕面街道]]がある。また、現在では旧街道に並行する、[[国道176号]]大阪 - 豊中 - 池田および[[国道173号]]池田 - 多田 - 能勢 - 篠山（ただしバイパス等を除く）の通称・別称として使われることも多い。 ==歴史と地理== 中津で[[中国街道]]から分岐して、[[十三 (大阪市)|十三]]、[[三国 (大阪市)|三国]]、[[庄内 (豊中市)|庄内]]、[[服部 (豊中市)|服部]]、[[岡町 (豊中市)|岡町]]、[[石橋 (大阪府)|石橋]]、池田、木部、古江、[[多田駅 (兵庫県)|多田]]、[[一の鳥居駅|一の鳥居]]、そして尾根筋を通り妙見山へ至る。 池田や[[能勢郡|能勢]]で産する酒や衣類、木材が当街道によって大坂へ運ばれ、さらには能勢から奥に続く[[丹波国]]の[[米]]、[[クリ|栗]]、[[炭]]、[[銀]]、[[銅]]などの搬出路でもあった。また終着地の能勢妙見をはじめ、沿道には[[服部天神宮]]、[[東光院 (豊中市)|東光院]]（萩の寺）、[[原田神社]]、[[多田神社]]など社寺が並び、街道の途中から入った[[中山寺 (宝塚市)|中山寺]]、[[勝尾寺]]などを含めての参拝路としても賑わいを見せた。明治9年（[[1876年]]）に太政官達60号で道路の種類および等級が定められた際、能勢街道は大阪府下で3本のみの一等縦貫線に掲げられている。 ところで岡町から池田まで、古くは刀根山（現在の[[大阪大学]]豊中キャンパス南方）回りの山道を通っていた。この坂が急で物資運搬に支障をきたすため、明治27年（[[1894年]]）に現在の阪急[[豊中駅]]付近から石橋にかけ、比較的平坦な新道が開かれた。これを「新能勢街道」といい、現在の[[国道176号]]ルートとほぼ同じである。 現在は大阪市から[[池田市]]まで[[阪急電鉄]][[阪急宝塚本線|宝塚線]]と国道176号が、池田市から妙見山麓まで[[国道173号]]および[[国道477号]]が並行している。また[[川西市]]以北では、[[能勢電鉄]][[能勢電鉄妙見線|妙見線]]が並行する。これら鉄道や道路の整備によって当街道は衰退したが、現在もかつての道筋の多くが残り、生活道路として利用されている。旧伊丹街道と交差する岡町付近や阪急電鉄宝塚線[[石橋阪大前駅]]周辺では商店街となっている。 [[阪神淡路大震災]]により、街道沿いの古い木造家屋は大きな打撃を受け、当時の街道を偲ぶ貴重な家屋も現在は建て替わったり駐車場などになり、かなり少なくなってしまった。 == 宿場一覧 == *[[服部 (豊中市)|服部]] *[[石橋 (大阪府)|石橋]]（瀬川宿） [[西国街道]]との結節点。 *池田 == 能勢街道を舞台にした作品 == *[[上方落語]]「[[池田の猪買い]]」（{{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=参照 |date=20011218151505}}） == 能勢街道沿道の名産 == *[[池田炭]] 切り口が菊のように美しいため「[[菊炭]]」と呼ばれているが池田がかつて集積場だったため「池田炭」とも呼ばれている。この炭は茶道用に使用される高級炭でかつては[[千利休]]も愛用したと言われる。 *[[日本酒|清酒]] 池田市の「[[呉春]]」は有名。 *[[猪肉]] かつて池田は猪の肉が名物であった。上方落語の「池田の猪買い」は大阪から池田に猪の買い入れにくる情景を描く。現在の北摂地域でもイノシシは生息するが、特産としての地位は失われた。 == 関連項目 == *[[国道176号]] *[[国道173号]] *[[妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)]] *[[能勢妙見山 (日蓮宗)]] ==外部リンク== *[http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ 服部天神宮] *[http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 近畿道ものがたり] ラジオ大阪サイト。 *[https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html 旧能勢街道岡町界隈]豊中市サイト {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{デフォルトソート:のせかいとう}} [[Category:街道]] [[Category:大阪府の交通史]] [[Category:兵庫県の交通史]] [[Category:大阪府の道路]] [[Category:兵庫県の道路]]

[[Image: Nose kaido&Harada shrine.jpg|thumbnail|240px|Nose Kaidō near Okamachi, which has become a shopping street. To the left is Harada Shrine.]] The '''Nose Kaidō''' (能勢街道) is an old highway stretching from {{ill|Nakatsu, Kita, Osaka|ja|中津 (大阪市)}} in [[Osaka]]'s Kita Ward, passing through {{ill|Ikeda, Osaka|ja|池田市}}, and ending at {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}}, where the {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)}} is located. From Chōkōji-minami, the highway splits, with the {{ill|Minoh Kaidō|ja|箕面街道}} providing a pilgrimage route to sites such as {{ill|Minoh Waterfall|ja|箕面の滝}}, {{ill|Ryuan-ji (Minoh)|ja|瀧安寺}}, and {{ill|Katsuo-ji|ja|勝尾寺}}. Today, the old road name is also used colloquially for the routes of {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} (Osaka–Toyonaka–Ikeda) and {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} (Ikeda–Tada–Nose–Sasayama), excluding bypasses. == History and Geography == The Nose Kaidō diverged from the {{ill|Chūgoku Kaidō|ja|中国街道}} in Nakatsu, then passed through {{ill|Jūso|ja|十三 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Mikuni, Osaka|ja|三国 (大阪市)}}, {{ill|Shōnai, Toyonaka|ja|庄内 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Okamachi, Toyonaka|ja|岡町 (豊中市)}}, {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}}, Ikeda, Kibe, Furue, {{ill|Tada Station (Hyogo)|ja|多田駅 (兵庫県)}}, and {{ill|Ichinotorii Station|ja|一の鳥居駅}}, before following the ridgeline to Myōken Mountain. This highway facilitated the transportation of goods such as sake, textiles, and timber from Ikeda and Nose to Osaka. Additionally, rice, chestnuts, charcoal, silver, and copper from {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国}} were carried along this route. Many significant religious sites lined the highway, including {{ill|Hattori Tenjingu Shrine|ja|服部天神宮}}, {{ill|Tōkō-in (Toyonaka)|ja|東光院 (豊中市)}} (Hagi Temple), {{ill|Harada Shrine (Toyonaka)|ja|原田神社}}, and {{ill|Tada Shrine|ja|多田神社}}. Other sites, such as {{ill|Nakayama-dera|ja|中山寺}} and Katsuo-ji, located off the main route, also attracted pilgrims. In 1876, the Nose Kaidō was designated as one of three first-class main roads in Osaka Prefecture under Daijōkan Order No. 60, which established road classifications. Historically, the route between Okamachi and Ikeda passed through Mount Tonoyama (south of Osaka University's Toyonaka Campus). Due to the steep incline, which hampered the transport of goods, a flatter "New Nose Kaidō" was built in 1894, running from the vicinity of present-day {{ill|Hankyu Toyonaka Station|ja|阪急豊中駅}} to Ishibashi. This new route closely aligns with today’s National Route 176. Modern infrastructure, such as {{ill|Hankyu Electric Railway|ja|阪急電鉄}}'s {{ill|Hankyu Takarazuka Line|ja|阪急宝塚本線}} and National Routes 176 and 173, run parallel to the old road. The {{ill|Nose Electric Railway|ja|能勢電鉄}}'s {{ill|Nose Electric Railway Myōken Line|ja|能勢電鉄妙見線}} also parallels the highway north of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyogo|ja|川西市}}. These developments led to the decline of the old highway, though much of its path remains as residential roads. Commercial hubs such as Okamachi and the area around {{ill|Ishibashi Handai-mae Station|ja|石橋阪大前駅}} have preserved the historical route as shopping streets. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')
({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == See Also == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Historic Roads]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Hyogo]] [[Category:Roads in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Roads in Hyogo Prefecture]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Nose Highway]] [[ja:能勢街道]] [[en:Nose Highway]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Nose Highway}} {{translated page|ja|能勢街道|version=102374857|comment=Imported full ja history}}

({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭|qq=draft}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休|qq=draft}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春|qq=draft}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == See Also == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号|qq=draft}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)|qq=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{translated page|ja|能勢街道|version=102374857|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nose Highway}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Historic Roads]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Hyogo]] [[Category:Roads in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Roads in Hyogo Prefecture]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Nose Highway]] [[ja:能勢街道]] [[en:Nose Highway]]

[[Image: Nose kaido&Harada shrine.jpg|thumbnail|240px|Nose Kaidō near Okamachi, which has become a shopping street. To the left is Harada Shrine.]] The '''Nose Kaidō''' (能勢街道) is an old highway stretching from {{ill|Nakatsu, Kita, Osaka|ja|中津 (大阪市)|qq=draft}} in [[Osaka]]'s Kita Ward, passing through {{ill|Ikeda, Osaka|ja|池田市|qq=draft}}, and ending at {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)|qq=draft}}, where the {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)|qq=draft}} is located. From Chōkōji-minami, the highway splits, with the {{ill|Minoh Kaidō|ja|箕面街道|qq=draft}} providing a pilgrimage route to sites such as {{ill|Minoh Waterfall|ja|箕面の滝|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ryuan-ji (Minoh)|ja|瀧安寺|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Katsuo-ji|ja|勝尾寺|qq=draft}}. Today, the old road name is also used colloquially for the routes of {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号|qq=draft}} (Osaka–Toyonaka–Ikeda) and {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号|qq=draft}} (Ikeda–Tada–Nose–Sasayama), excluding bypasses. == History and Geography == The Nose Kaidō diverged from the {{ill|Chūgoku Kaidō|ja|中国街道|qq=draft}} in Nakatsu, then passed through {{ill|Jūso|ja|十三 (大阪市)|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Mikuni, Osaka|ja|三国 (大阪市)|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shōnai, Toyonaka|ja|庄内 (豊中市)|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Okamachi, Toyonaka|ja|岡町 (豊中市)|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)|qq=draft}}, Ikeda, Kibe, Furue, {{ill|Tada Station (Hyogo)|ja|多田駅 (兵庫県)|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Ichinotorii Station|ja|一の鳥居駅|qq=draft}}, before following the ridgeline to Myōken Mountain. This highway facilitated the transportation of goods such as sake, textiles, and timber from Ikeda and Nose to Osaka. Additionally, rice, chestnuts, charcoal, silver, and copper from {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国|qq=draft}} were carried along this route. Many significant religious sites lined the highway, including {{ill|Hattori Tenjingu Shrine|ja|服部天神宮|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tōkō-in (Toyonaka)|ja|東光院 (豊中市)|qq=draft}} (Hagi Temple), {{ill|Harada Shrine (Toyonaka)|ja|原田神社|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Tada Shrine|ja|多田神社|qq=draft}}. Other sites, such as {{ill|Nakayama-dera|ja|中山寺|qq=draft}} and Katsuo-ji, located off the main route, also attracted pilgrims. In 1876, the Nose Kaidō was designated as one of three first-class main roads in Osaka Prefecture under Daijōkan Order No. 60, which established road classifications. Historically, the route between Okamachi and Ikeda passed through Mount Tonoyama (south of Osaka University's Toyonaka Campus). Due to the steep incline, which hampered the transport of goods, a flatter "New Nose Kaidō" was built in 1894, running from the vicinity of present-day {{ill|Hankyu Toyonaka Station|ja|阪急豊中駅|qq=draft}} to Ishibashi. This new route closely aligns with today’s National Route 176. Modern infrastructure, such as {{ill|Hankyu Electric Railway|ja|阪急電鉄|qq=draft}}'s {{ill|Hankyu Takarazuka Line|ja|阪急宝塚本線|qq=draft}} and National Routes 176 and 173, run parallel to the old road. The {{ill|Nose Electric Railway|ja|能勢電鉄|qq=draft}}'s {{ill|Nose Electric Railway Myōken Line|ja|能勢電鉄妙見線|qq=draft}} also parallels the highway north of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyogo|ja|川西市|qq=draft}}. These developments led to the decline of the old highway, though much of its path remains as residential roads. Commercial hubs such as Okamachi and the area around {{ill|Ishibashi Handai-mae Station|ja|石橋阪大前駅|qq=draft}} have preserved the historical route as shopping streets. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災|qq=draft}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)|qq=draft}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道|qq=draft}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い|qq=draft}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')
({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭|qq=draft}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休|qq=draft}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春|qq=draft}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == See Also == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号|qq=draft}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)|qq=draft}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{translated page|ja|能勢街道|version=102374857|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Historic Roads]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Hyogo]] [[Category:Roads in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Roads in Hyogo Prefecture]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Nose Highway]] [[ja:能勢街道]] [[en:Nose Highway]]

({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikuzumi|qq=}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sen no Rikyū|qq=}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshun|qq=}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == See Also == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Route 176 (Japan)|qq=}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Route 173 (Japan)|qq=}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyogo)|qq=}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{translated page|ja|能勢街道|version=102374857|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Historic Roads]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Hyogo]] [[Category:Roads in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Roads in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Nose Highway]] [[ja:能勢街道]] [[en:Nose Highway]]

[[Image: Nose kaido&Harada shrine.jpg|thumbnail|240px|Nose Kaidō near Okamachi, which has become a shopping street. To the left is Harada Shrine.]] The '''Nose Kaidō''' (能勢街道) is an old highway stretching from {{ill|Nakatsu, Kita, Osaka|ja|中津 (大阪市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakatsu, Kita, Osaka|qq=}} in [[Osaka]]'s Kita Ward, passing through {{ill|Ikeda, Osaka|ja|池田市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikeda, Osaka|qq=}}, and ending at {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyōgo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyōgo)|qq=}}, where the {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|qq=}} is located. From Chōkōji-minami, the highway splits, with the {{ill|Minoh Kaidō|ja|箕面街道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minoh Kaidō|qq=}} providing a pilgrimage route to sites such as {{ill|Minoh Waterfall|ja|箕面の滝|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minoh Waterfall|qq=}}, {{ill|Ryuan-ji (Minoh)|ja|瀧安寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryuan-ji (Minoh)|qq=}}, and {{ill|Katsuo-ji|ja|勝尾寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuo-ji|qq=}}. Today, the old road name is also used colloquially for the routes of {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Route 176 (Japan)|qq=}} (Osaka–Toyonaka–Ikeda) and {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Route 173 (Japan)|qq=}} (Ikeda–Tada–Nose–Sasayama), excluding bypasses. == History and Geography == The Nose Kaidō diverged from the {{ill|Chūgoku Kaidō|ja|中国街道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūgoku Kaidō|qq=}} in Nakatsu, then passed through {{ill|Jūso|ja|十三 (大阪市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūso|qq=}}, {{ill|Mikuni, Osaka|ja|三国 (大阪市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikuni, Osaka|qq=}}, {{ill|Shōnai, Toyonaka|ja|庄内 (豊中市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōnai, Toyonaka|qq=}}, {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hattori, Toyonaka|qq=}}, {{ill|Okamachi, Toyonaka|ja|岡町 (豊中市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okamachi, Toyonaka|qq=}}, {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishibashi, Osaka|qq=}}, Ikeda, Kibe, Furue, {{ill|Tada Station (Hyōgo)|ja|多田駅 (兵庫県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tada Station (Hyōgo)|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ichinotorii Station|ja|一の鳥居駅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichinotorii Station|qq=}}, before following the ridgeline to Myōken Mountain. This highway facilitated the transportation of goods such as sake, textiles, and timber from Ikeda and Nose to Osaka. Additionally, rice, chestnuts, charcoal, silver, and copper from {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Province|qq=}} were carried along this route. Many significant religious sites lined the highway, including {{ill|Hattori Tenjingu Shrine|ja|服部天神宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hattori Tenjingu Shrine|qq=}}, {{ill|Tōkō-in (Toyonaka)|ja|東光院 (豊中市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkō-in (Toyonaka)|qq=}} (Hagi Temple), {{ill|Harada Shrine (Toyonaka)|ja|原田神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harada Shrine (Toyonaka)|qq=}}, and {{ill|Tada Shrine|ja|多田神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tada Shrine|qq=}}. Other sites, such as {{ill|Nakayama-dera|ja|中山寺|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nakayama-dera|qq=}} and Katsuo-ji, located off the main route, also attracted pilgrims. In 1876, the Nose Kaidō was designated as one of three first-class main roads in Osaka Prefecture under Daijōkan Order No. 60, which established road classifications. Historically, the route between Okamachi and Ikeda passed through Mount Tonoyama (south of Osaka University's Toyonaka Campus). Due to the steep incline, which hampered the transport of goods, a flatter "New Nose Kaidō" was built in 1894, running from the vicinity of present-day {{ill|Hankyu Toyonaka Station|ja|阪急豊中駅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hankyu Toyonaka Station|qq=}} to Ishibashi. This new route closely aligns with today’s National Route 176. Modern infrastructure, such as {{ill|Hankyu Electric Railway|ja|阪急電鉄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hankyu Electric Railway|qq=}}'s {{ill|Hankyu Takarazuka Line|ja|阪急宝塚本線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hankyu Takarazuka Line|qq=}} and National Routes 176 and 173, run parallel to the old road. The {{ill|Nose Electric Railway|ja|能勢電鉄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nose Electric Railway|qq=}}'s {{ill|Nose Electric Railway Myōken Line|ja|能勢電鉄妙見線|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nose Electric Railway Myōken Line|qq=}} also parallels the highway north of {{ill|Kawanishi, Hyōgo|ja|川西市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kawanishi, Hyōgo|qq=}}. These developments led to the decline of the old highway, though much of its path remains as residential roads. Commercial hubs such as Okamachi and the area around {{ill|Ishibashi Handai-mae Station|ja|石橋阪大前駅|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishibashi Handai-mae Station|qq=}} have preserved the historical route as shopping streets. The {{ill|Great Hanshin Earthquake|ja|阪神淡路大震災|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Great Hanshin Earthquake|qq=}} severely damaged many old wooden buildings along the route. Unfortunately, some of the historic architecture was replaced or demolished, and many sites have been converted into parking lots. == Inns Along the Highway == * {{ill|Hattori, Toyonaka|ja|服部 (豊中市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hattori, Toyonaka|qq=}} * {{ill|Ishibashi, Osaka|ja|石橋 (大阪府)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishibashi, Osaka|qq=}} (Segawa-shuku), a junction with the {{ill|Saigoku Kaidō|ja|西国街道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saigoku Kaidō|qq=}} * Ikeda == Works Featuring Nose Kaidō == * The [[rakugo]] story "{{ill|Ikeda no Inokai|ja|池田の猪買い|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikeda no Inokai|qq=}}" (''Buying Wild Boar in Ikeda'')
({{Wayback|url=http://www.geocities.co.jp/Hollywood/2975/sub16.html |title=Referenced |date=20011218151505}}). == Famous Products Along the Nose Kaidō == * '''Ikeda Charcoal''' – Known for its chrysanthemum-like cross-section, called "{{ill|Kikuzumi|ja|菊炭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kikuzumi|qq=}}" for tea ceremonies. It was once favored by {{ill|Sen no Rikyū|ja|千利休|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sen no Rikyū|qq=}}. * '''Sake''' – Ikeda’s "{{ill|Goshun|ja|呉春|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goshun|qq=}}" is especially renowned. * '''Boar Meat''' – Ikeda was once famous for boar meat, as featured in the rakugo story "Ikeda no Inokai." Although wild boars still inhabit the region, the meat has lost its status as a local specialty. == See Also == * {{ill|National Route 176 (Japan)|ja|国道176号|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Route 176 (Japan)|qq=}} * {{ill|National Route 173 (Japan)|ja|国道173号|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Route 173 (Japan)|qq=}} * {{ill|Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyōgo)|ja|妙見山 (大阪府・兵庫県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Myōken (Osaka and Hyōgo)|qq=}} * {{ill|Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|ja|能勢妙見山 (Nichiren宗)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nose Myōkendo (Nichiren-shū)|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://www.apsara.ne.jp/hattori-tenjingu/ Hattori Tenjingu Shrine] * [http://con-bat.sakura.ne.jp/michi-kawa/album/index2.html#1 Kinki Road Stories] (Radio Osaka site) * [https://www.city.toyonaka.osaka.jp/machi/toshikeikan/hyakei/field/omomuki/kyunosekaidou.html Old Nose Kaidō near Okamachi] (Toyonaka City site) {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{translated page|ja|能勢街道|version=102374857|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Historic Roads]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Osaka]] [[Category:History of Transportation in Hyōgo]] [[Category:Roads in Osaka Prefecture]] [[Category:Roads in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Nose Highway]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[sn:Nose Highway]] [[ja:能勢街道]] [[en:Nose Highway]]
